column1: prediction

{"root cause occurrence datetime": "2024-11-18 05:34:15", "root cause component": "productcatalogservice", "root cause reason": "Timeline analysis reveals the earliest anomalous signal in the entire dependency graph is a workload spike on productcatalogservice at 05:34:15 (workload: 78.9), which predates all other service anomalies. Within seconds, shippingservice (05:34:22) and adservice (05:34:23) also show elevated memory \u2014 consistent with a cascade triggered by a shared upstream load burst. currencyservice follows at 05:36:04. The pattern of memory pressure propagating outward across multiple downstream services in rapid succession strongly suggests productcatalogservice became overloaded first, causing dependent services to queue, retry, or accumulate in-flight requests, which drove their memory consumption up. By 05:45\u201305:52, mid-tier services (frontend, checkoutservice, recommendationservice, emailservice) show degraded memory metrics. The cartservice then begins logging Grpc.AspNetCore.Server.ServerCallHandler[7] faults at 05:54:33 \u2014 a gRPC server-side error indicating it is failing to serve requests, likely due to upstream dependency exhaustion from productcatalogservice or redis-cart (redis-cart shows elevated disk I/O at 05:51:32, suggesting persistence pressure). Frontend error rate spikes at 05:54:31 and request errors begin at 05:55:00, confirming the user-facing impact is the terminal symptom of this cascade, not the origin. The absence of any deployment or config change signals in the data means the most probable trigger is an organic workload surge or a resource exhaustion event (e.g., CPU/thread saturation) on productcatalogservice around 05:34:15, possibly exacerbated by an upstream traffic spike or a slow/hanging catalog query that held connections open across the dependency graph."}

{"root cause occurrence datetime": "2024-11-22 12:56:47", "root cause component": "paymentservice", "root cause reason": "While multiple services show elevated memory metrics beginning as early as 12:39:51 (productcatalogservice), those memory readings are consistent with normal operational baselines and do not by themselves indicate a fault \u2014 they appear to be routine telemetry collected across the fleet. The critical distinguishing signal is the explicit application-level error logged in paymentservice at 12:56:47: 'UnacceptedCreditCard [Error]: Sorry, we cannot process visa_electron credit cards. Only VISA or MasterCard is accepted.' This is a hard business-logic failure originating inside charge.js at line 74, indicating that a code change or configuration update introduced a stricter card-type validation rule that now rejects visa_electron cards \u2014 a card type that was presumably accepted previously. This paymentservice fault is the likely trigger for the observed cascade: (1) checkout flows invoking paymentservice begin failing, causing checkoutservice (memory spike at 12:55:36, slightly before the logged error \u2014 consistent with retry storms building up) to accumulate failed requests; (2) cartservice starts logging gRPC server handler faults at 13:01:50\u201313:01:59 as upstream checkout retries propagate back through the cart dependency; (3) frontend accumulates error responses and by 12:57:54 reaches a 100% error rate, with error logs firing from 13:02:19 onward. The redis-cart disk I/O spike at 12:44:48 and memory at 12:58:45 are consistent with retry-driven read amplification on the cart. adservice and recommendationservice memory readings are unremarkable and likely coincidental. The root cause is a breaking change in paymentservice charge logic (charge.js) that disallows visa_electron, most plausibly introduced via a recent deployment or PR modifying card validation logic."}

{"root cause occurrence datetime": "2024-11-23 13:23:56", "root cause component": "productcatalogservice", "root cause reason": "Analyzing the chronological sequence of anomalous events across all services reveals a propagation pattern consistent with an upstream dependency failure. The earliest anomalous signal in the dataset is productcatalogservice reporting an elevated workload metric of 72.2 at 13:23:56, followed closely by redis-cart showing elevated disk I/O (123,465) at 13:24:26. These two early signals precede all other memory spikes across the fleet. The cascading memory pressure then propagates in temporal order: currencyservice mem spike at 13:19:27 (notably this timestamp is earlier, suggesting it may be a coincidental baseline anomaly or clock skew, but lacks corroborating signals), redis-cart mem at 13:30:51, shippingservice at 13:32:24, paymentservice at 13:32:43, frontend mem at 13:32:58, cartservice mem at 13:34:51, emailservice at 13:35:00, checkoutservice at 13:37:10, recommendationservice at 13:37:25, productcatalogservice mem at 13:37:34, and finally adservice mem and frontend error rate elevating at 13:42:29. The cartservice gRPC handler faults (Grpc.AspNetCore.Server.ServerCallHandler[7]) appearing at 13:41:56\u201313:41:59 are consistent with upstream call failures from a saturated productcatalogservice, as cartservice depends on productcatalogservice for product lookups. The frontend request errors beginning at 13:42:21 represent the user-visible tail of this propagation chain. The productcatalogservice workload spike at 13:23:56 is the earliest leading indicator of resource exhaustion \u2014 likely triggered by a sudden traffic surge, a hot-loop bug introduced via a recent deployment, or a missing pagination/cache layer causing full catalog scans. This workload saturation would increase response latency and memory consumption, causing dependent services to queue requests, exhaust connection pools, and eventually surface gRPC and HTTP errors upstream to the frontend. Confidence is moderate (0.62) because no explicit deployment event, config change, or code diff is present in the provided data, and the currencyservice early timestamp introduces some ambiguity in the precise origin."}

{"root cause occurrence datetime": "2024-11-18 06:06:20", "root cause component": "paymentservice", "root cause reason": "While memory anomalies appear across multiple services starting as early as 06:00 (cartservice, adservice, frontend), these are likely symptomatic of a broader resource pressure event rather than the originating fault. The single most actionable and semantically meaningful error in the dataset is the explicit application-level exception in paymentservice at 06:06:20: 'UnacceptedCreditCard [Error]: Sorry, we cannot process visa_electron credit cards. Only VISA or MasterCard is accepted.' This is a hard business logic failure at charge.js:74, indicating a code-level defect \u2014 most likely introduced via a recent deployment or config change to paymentservice that either (a) tightened card validation logic without accounting for existing accepted card types, or (b) introduced a regression in the allowlist of accepted card networks. This payment failure would cascade upstream: checkoutservice calls paymentservice during order completion; failures there cause checkout transactions to fail and retry loops to accumulate, driving memory pressure in checkoutservice (anomaly at 06:02:17, though slightly earlier \u2014 possibly pre-existing load or a staggered rollout). The frontend begins surfacing request errors at 06:20:46 with an elevated error rate of 5.9 at 06:19:46, consistent with downstream propagation from checkout/payment failures reaching the user-facing layer ~14 minutes after the payment error. The disk I/O spike on redis-cart at 06:11:49 is consistent with cart operations being retried or stalled due to failed checkout flows. The widespread memory anomalies across nearly all services at ~06:00\u201306:08 may indicate a rolling restart or deployment wave that introduced the faulty paymentservice build. No direct deployment artifact is visible in the data, but the charge.js stack trace strongly implies a code change to payment validation logic is the trigger. Confidence is moderated (0.72) because the earliest timestamps predate the payment error, leaving open the possibility that a broader infrastructure event (e.g., a bad deployment rollout or resource exhaustion) is the true root cause, with paymentservice being the most visible symptom."}

{"root cause occurrence datetime": "2024-11-22 13:09:43", "root cause component": "paymentservice", "root cause reason": "While memory anomalies began appearing across the fleet as early as 13:06:19 (productcatalogservice) suggesting a broader resource pressure event, the only service exhibiting a concrete, application-level error with a clear causal signature is paymentservice. At 13:09:43, paymentservice logged an explicit business-logic failure: 'UnacceptedCreditCard [Error]: Sorry, we cannot process visa_electron credit cards. Only VISA or MasterCard is accepted' originating from charge.js:74. This is a code-level defect \u2014 likely introduced by a recent deployment or configuration change to charge.js \u2014 that causes payment processing to throw an unhandled exception for visa_electron card types. The propagation path is as follows: (1) paymentservice begins rejecting valid payment attempts at 13:09:43, causing checkout flows to fail; (2) checkoutservice (which depends on paymentservice) begins accumulating errors and memory pressure by 13:16:13; (3) cartservice, which feeds into checkout, shows memory anomalies from 13:07:31, possibly an independent resource spike or early cascade; (4) the failure propagates upstream through recommendationservice, currencyservice, adservice, shippingservice, emailservice, and redis-cart as cascading timeouts and retries exhaust resources; (5) frontend, the terminal user-facing layer, begins surfacing elevated error rates (5.3) at 13:21:23 and a burst of request errors at 13:28:06-07. The specificity of the paymentservice stack trace pointing to a hardcoded card-type allowlist in charge.js strongly implies a code change (PR merge or direct deploy) modified the accepted card type logic, removing visa_electron support. The memory spikes across unrelated services starting before 13:09:43 may indicate a separate rolling restart or node-level memory event occurring concurrently, but the user-visible failure chain is anchored to the paymentservice charge logic defect."}

{"root cause occurrence datetime": "2024-11-23 13:55:54", "root cause component": "paymentservice", "root cause reason": "The most semantically significant and actionable signal in this incident is the explicit application-level error logged by paymentservice at 13:55:54: 'UnacceptedCreditCard [Error]: Sorry, we cannot process visa_electron credit cards. Only VISA or MasterCard is accepted.' This is a hard business logic failure with a clear stack trace pointing to charge.js:74, indicating either a recent code change, a configuration update to accepted card types, or a data-driven trigger (e.g., a new card type being passed by upstream callers). While memory anomalies are visible across nearly all services \u2014 with the earliest spike observed in cartservice at 13:47:38 and redis-cart disk I/O anomaly at 13:44:31 \u2014 these are likely symptoms of cascading retry storms and connection pool exhaustion rather than root causes in themselves. The paymentservice error is the only event with a concrete failure reason and code-level attribution. The propagation path is consistent with a checkout flow failure: paymentservice rejects the charge \u2192 checkoutservice receives errors and retries \u2192 cartservice and redis-cart experience elevated load \u2192 frontend accumulates errors (logged at 14:08:05 as multiple request errors, well after the payment failure). The elevated memory across all services is consistent with retry amplification across the dependency graph. The redis-cart disk I/O spike at 13:44:31 is notable as the earliest timestamp and may indicate a pre-existing stress condition or a separate contributing factor, but without a corresponding error log it is treated as circumstantial. The primary actionable root cause remains the paymentservice charge logic rejecting visa_electron card types."}

{"root cause occurrence datetime": "2024-11-18 06:34:49", "root cause component": "emailservice", "root cause reason": "The failure chain points clearly to emailservice as Patient Zero. The earliest anomalous events in the entire incident timeline are code faults on emailservice starting at 06:34:49 \u2014 a recurring 'AttributeError: _Server object has no attribute Check', which is a well-known gRPC health-checking misconfiguration error in Python gRPC servers (typically caused by a dependency version mismatch or a bad deployment where the grpcio-health-checking package is incompatible or missing). These tracebacks repeat five times between 06:34:49 and 06:36:48, indicating a persistent crash-loop or repeated failed health probe attempts. The emailservice memory metric also spikes at 06:35:13, consistent with a process in a degraded/restarting state. The downstream blast radius is then observable: checkoutservice begins logging 'rpc error: code = Unavailable desc = no healthy upstream' errors at 06:41:29 \u2014 directly referencing its inability to reach emailservice for order confirmation sends. This is the canonical propagation path: emailservice crashes \u2192 its gRPC endpoint becomes unavailable \u2192 checkoutservice cannot complete order confirmation RPCs \u2192 checkout failures degrade the overall transaction flow \u2192 frontend begins accumulating errors (error rate 0.1 at 06:44:16). The memory anomalies across other services (currencyservice at 06:25:07, recommendationservice at 06:25:58) predate the emailservice fault but lack associated error logs, suggesting those may be pre-existing elevated-but-stable baselines or unrelated noise rather than causal contributors. The smoking gun is the specific AttributeError on emailservice, which indicates a code or dependency regression \u2014 most likely a recent deployment that introduced an incompatible grpcio version or removed the health servicer registration from the gRPC server initialization."}

{"root cause occurrence datetime": "2024-11-22 13:42:15", "root cause component": "emailservice", "root cause reason": "The failure chain is best explained by emailservice as the origin of the cascading outage, with the earliest definitive code-level fault. Here is the full reasoning:

1. EARLIEST HARD FAULT — emailservice: At 13:42:15, emailservice begins emitting repeated Python tracebacks with 'AttributeError: _Server object has no attribute Check'. This is a well-known gRPC health-check misconfiguration error — the grpc.health.v1 HealthServicer was not properly added to the server, causing the health probe to fail. This is a classic symptom of a bad deployment (e.g., a Python dependency version mismatch or a code change that removed the health check registration). The error repeats at 13:42:23, 13:42:43, 13:43:19, and 13:44:10, confirming a persistent crash loop, not a transient blip.

2. PROPAGATION TO checkoutservice: At 13:48:05–13:48:22, checkoutservice logs 'rpc error: code = Unavailable desc = no healthy upstream' when attempting to send order confirmation emails. This is a direct downstream consequence of emailservice being unhealthy — the gRPC load balancer has no healthy upstream to route to, because emailservice's health check is failing.

3. TIMELINE OF MEMORY ANOMALIES: Memory usage spikes are visible across nearly all services starting from ~13:29:59 (frontend) through to 13:53:03 (adservice). These broad memory anomalies across unrelated services suggest a platform-level event (e.g., a noisy-neighbor memory pressure, node-level issue, or a rolling deployment) around 13:29–13:35, which likely triggered the emailservice deployment that introduced the regression. The emailservice memory metric at 13:44:07 is consistent with a service that is crash-looping and accumulating memory before GC.

4. paymentservice error: The UnacceptedCreditCard error at 13:50:08 is an independent application-logic issue (visa_electron card type not supported), not structurally related to the emailservice fault. It may represent a secondary user-impacting issue but is not the root cause of the systemic outage.

5. ROOT CAUSE CONCLUSION: emailservice received a deployment (likely between 13:35–13:42) that introduced a gRPC health check registration bug — either a missing add_HealthServicer_to_server() call or an incompatible grpcio-health-checking library version. This caused emailservice to fail all health probes, become marked unhealthy by the service mesh/load balancer, and produce 'no healthy upstream' errors for checkoutservice, surfacing as order confirmation failures to end users. The broader memory pressure across the cluster starting at ~13:30 is the likely trigger for the deployment rollout that introduced this regression."}

{"root cause occurrence datetime": "2024-11-23 14:22:12", "root cause component": "emailservice", "root cause reason": "The evidence strongly points to emailservice as Patient Zero. The earliest actionable fault signals in the entire incident are the repeated 'AttributeError: _Server object has no attribute Check' exceptions in emailservice, beginning at 14:22:12 \u2014 predating all other service error logs. This specific Python AttributeError is a well-known symptom of a gRPC health-checking misconfiguration or a version incompatibility introduced by a dependency upgrade (e.g., grpcio or grpcio-health-checking package mismatch), where the health check server object no longer exposes the expected 'Check' interface. The tracebacks repeat at 14:22:12, 14:22:19, 14:22:38, 14:23:10, and 14:24:07, indicating the service entered a crash-loop or degraded state immediately. This caused emailservice to become unhealthy and unable to respond to RPCs. The propagation chain is clear: checkoutservice depends on emailservice to send order confirmation emails. Starting at 14:27:13, checkoutservice begins logging 'rpc error: code = Unavailable desc = no healthy upstream' \u2014 the direct downstream consequence of emailservice being unreachable. The 'no healthy upstream' gRPC status confirms the upstream (emailservice) failed its health check, which is precisely what the AttributeError on '_Server.Check' would cause. The broader memory anomalies across frontend, adservice, cartservice, currencyservice, recommendationservice, shippingservice, redis-cart, paymentservice, and productcatalogservice are consistent with cascading load redistribution, retry storms, and connection pool exhaustion spreading outward from the broken email/checkout flow. The productcatalogservice workload spike at 14:16:56 and currencyservice memory anomaly at 14:13:27 are earlier in timestamp but represent metric anomalies without associated error logs, suggesting they may be pre-existing elevated load rather than fault origin. The first hard code fault with a concrete, reproducible error is emailservice at 14:22:12. The most likely trigger is an unintentional deployment or dependency version bump of the grpcio-health-checking library in the emailservice container image, breaking the health check interface contract."}

{"root cause occurrence datetime": "2024-11-18 07:00:32", "root cause component": "emailservice", "root cause reason": "The evidence points clearly to emailservice as Patient Zero for the checkout-path failure chain. Starting at 07:00:32, emailservice began emitting repeated Python tracebacks alongside a deterministic TypeError: 'add_HealthServicer_to_server() missing 1 required positional argument: server'. This is not a transient runtime error \u2014 it is a code-level defect, almost certainly introduced by a bad deployment or dependency version bump that broke the gRPC health servicer registration call signature. Because the health check endpoint fails to initialize correctly, the emailservice process never registers as healthy with the service mesh/load balancer, causing it to report 'no healthy upstream' to any caller. This directly explains the cascade observed in checkoutservice: beginning at 07:06:03, checkoutservice logs a burst of 'rpc error: code = Unavailable desc = no healthy upstream' errors when attempting to dispatch order confirmation emails \u2014 exactly what you would expect when emailservice's gRPC health probe is broken and the upstream is culled from the pool. The broader memory pressure seen across frontend (06:50:32), currencyservice (06:51:07), redis-cart (06:52:38), paymentservice (06:55:38), cartservice (06:56:32), and others is consistent with a system-wide resource anomaly or a rolling deployment wave that preceded the emailservice crash loop, but none of those services exhibit the specific, deterministic code fault that emailservice does. The paymentservice visa_electron rejection at 07:11:22 is an independent application-logic issue (unsupported card type) and is not part of this failure chain. The root cause is a breaking API change in the gRPC health servicer registration \u2014 likely a grpcio-health-checking library version upgrade that changed the function signature of add_HealthServicer_to_server(), requiring a 'server' argument that the service code is not passing."}

{"root cause occurrence datetime": "2024-11-22 14:07:54", "root cause component": "emailservice", "root cause reason": "The evidence strongly points to emailservice as Patient Zero. The earliest definitively actionable fault signals appear at 2024-11-22 14:07:54 in the form of repeated Python tracebacks and a consistent TypeError: add_HealthServicer_to_server() missing 1 required positional argument: 'server'. This is a code-level bug \u2014 almost certainly introduced by a bad deployment \u2014 where the gRPC health servicer is being registered incorrectly, causing the service to crash-loop and never successfully register as a healthy upstream. The error fires 5 times between 14:07:54 and 14:09:51, confirming a persistent crash-loop rather than a transient fault. The causal chain then propagates as follows: (1) emailservice enters a crash-loop and becomes unavailable as a healthy gRPC upstream; (2) checkoutservice, which calls emailservice to send order confirmation emails, begins logging 'rpc error: code = Unavailable desc = no healthy upstream' starting at 14:14:34 \u2014 approximately 6\u20137 minutes after emailservice began failing, consistent with retry/backoff exhaustion; (3) frontend begins surfacing elevated error rates (0.9 at 14:13:54) as checkout flows fail end-to-end; (4) the memory anomalies observed across adservice, cartservice, recommendationservice, redis-cart, etc. are likely pre-existing elevated baselines or collateral pressure from retry storms, not independent root causes \u2014 notably redis-cart's earliest anomaly at 13:56:23 may reflect baseline noise or a separate minor issue, but it has no log-level fault evidence and no downstream error chain tied to it. The TypeError itself is a signature of an API contract mismatch \u2014 likely a grpcio or grpcio-health-checking library version bump in a recent deployment where the function signature for add_HealthServicer_to_server changed, or alternatively a code change that incorrectly calls the function with a missing argument."}

{"root cause occurrence datetime": "2024-11-23 14:47:52", "root cause component": "emailservice", "root cause reason": "The evidence strongly points to emailservice as Patient Zero for the cascading failure. The smoking gun is a repeated, deterministic code fault: 'TypeError: add_HealthServicer_to_server() missing 1 required positional argument: server'. This is not a transient runtime error \u2014 it is a Python TypeError indicating a breaking API contract violation, almost certainly introduced by a bad deployment or dependency version bump that changed the gRPC health servicer registration signature. This crash loop began at 14:47:52 and repeated consistently through 14:49:38, meaning the emailservice was unable to initialize its gRPC health check endpoint and was therefore reporting as unhealthy to the service mesh. The downstream blast radius is clearly visible: checkoutservice began logging 'rpc error: code = Unavailable desc = no healthy upstream' starting at 14:53:49 \u2014 approximately 6 minutes after emailservice began crashing, consistent with retry exhaustion and circuit breaker trip times. The checkoutservice depends on emailservice to dispatch order confirmation emails; with emailservice perpetually crash-looping and failing health probes, checkoutservice's outbound RPC calls found no healthy upstream. This in turn degraded frontend, which surfaced an error rate of 1.9 at 14:38:49 \u2014 notably this timestamp is earlier, suggesting frontend was already under stress from other elevated memory conditions across the fleet (redis-cart anomaly at 14:35:42, paymentservice at 14:35:53), but the critical user-facing checkout flow failure is directly attributable to the emailservice code fault. The other services showing elevated memory (adservice, cartservice, recommendationservice, currencyservice, etc.) are consistent with a broader load or memory pressure event, but none exhibit the deterministic, repeated code-level fault signature that emailservice does. The TypeError is the clearest causal signal in the dataset. The likely trigger is a recent deployment of emailservice that introduced an incompatible version of the grpcio-health-checking library or modified the HealthServicer initialization call without passing the required 'server' argument."}

{"root cause occurrence datetime": "2024-11-18 04:14:43", "root cause component": "cartservice", "root cause reason": "Chronological analysis of all anomalous events reveals cartservice as the earliest exhibiting a fault signal, with a memory anomaly recorded at 04:14:43 \u2014 the oldest timestamp across all services. The failure propagation timeline is as follows: (1) cartservice memory spike at 04:14:43 suggests an initial resource exhaustion or leak, likely affecting its dependency redis-cart and upstream callers such as checkoutservice and frontend; (2) productcatalogservice anomaly follows at 04:15:35, consistent with cascading load redistribution or retry storms; (3) shippingservice at 04:23:46; (4) frontend error rate elevates at 04:24:31, indicating user-facing impact is now measurable; (5) checkoutservice at 04:26:14; (6) adservice error rate peaks at 0.9 at 04:26:31 \u2014 a high error rate suggesting adservice itself may be a co-contributor or secondary victim; (7) redis-cart and currencyservice show anomalies around 04:30\u201304:31, consistent with downstream pressure from cartservice degradation; (8) frontend begins logging repeated 'failed to retrieve ads' errors at 04:37:48\u201304:37:50, confirming both adservice and the broader checkout/cart flow are degraded. The adservice high error rate (0.9) and large memory footprint (169 MB) make it a strong secondary suspect, and it may be contributing independently to frontend failures. However, the cartservice anomaly predates all others by approximately 8\u201312 minutes, making it the most likely Patient Zero. No explicit deployment or config change event is present in the provided data, but the abrupt memory spike pattern in cartservice is consistent with a recent rollout introducing a memory leak or a misconfigured connection pool exhausting redis-cart. Confidence is moderate (0.62) given the absence of explicit change markers and the possibility that adservice degradation is a parallel independent fault."}

{"root cause occurrence datetime": "2024-11-22 11:33:39", "root cause component": "adservice", "root cause reason": "The failure chain is anchored by adservice, which exhibits the earliest and most severe anomalous signals in the dataset. At 11:33:39, adservice recorded an error rate of 1.1 \u2014 a critically elevated figure that stands out as the first hard fault signal across all services. This was followed by a memory spike to 165MB at 11:37:43, consistent with a service under stress (e.g., stuck goroutines, retry storms, or a memory leak triggered by a bad deployment). The causal chain is clear: frontend began logging 'failed to retrieve ads' at 11:45:09\u201311:45:11, which is a direct downstream consequence of adservice being unhealthy. Frontend's own error rate anomaly appeared at 11:35:08 \u2014 approximately 90 seconds after adservice's error rate spiked \u2014 consistent with propagation latency and retry buffering. The remaining services (cartservice, checkoutservice, currencyservice, productcatalogservice, recommendationservice, shippingservice, redis-cart, emailservice, paymentservice) all show memory anomalies but no error rate spikes, suggesting they are experiencing elevated resource pressure (likely due to retry storms or increased load redistribution) rather than being originators of failure. redis-cart shows a disk I/O spike at 11:31:23, which is notable and slightly earlier; however, no error rate signal exists for redis-cart and it serves cartservice, not adservice or frontend's ad retrieval path, making it a coincidental or secondary observation. productcatalogservice shows a workload spike at 11:25:30, but again no error rate, suggesting it was under elevated load before the incident but not the failure origin. The convergence of the earliest error rate signal at adservice, the direct 'failed to retrieve ads' errors in frontend, and the temporal ordering of events strongly points to adservice as Patient Zero."}

{"root cause occurrence datetime": "2024-11-23 12:13:34", "root cause component": "adservice", "root cause reason": "The failure chain points to adservice as Patient Zero. The earliest anomalous signal with a clear fault indicator is adservice's error rate spiking to 0.9 (90%) at 12:13:34 \u2014 an exceptionally high error rate that stands out against all other services, which only report elevated memory usage without corresponding error rate spikes. This is the only service exhibiting both a high error rate AND elevated memory usage (171081728 bytes, the largest memory footprint in the cluster at 171MB), suggesting a memory pressure or resource exhaustion scenario causing request failures. The propagation path is straightforward: frontend depends on adservice for ad retrieval, and the cascade is evidenced by the repeated 'failed to retrieve ads' error logs on frontend beginning at 12:25:07 \u2014 approximately 11 minutes after adservice's error rate alarm, consistent with retry/timeout exhaustion and circuit-breaker delay. The frontend's own error rate anomaly at 12:04:44 is noted as earlier, but it is mild (0.1 = 10%) and likely represents pre-existing baseline noise or an unrelated upstream blip, whereas the 90% error rate on adservice is definitively fault-level. redis-cart shows an early memory signal at 12:09:12 and a disk I/O spike at 12:19:20, which may indicate general cluster resource pressure but does not directly explain the ad retrieval failures. The remaining services (cartservice, checkoutservice, currencyservice, productcatalogservice, recommendationservice, shippingservice, emailservice, paymentservice) show only memory metrics with no error signals, suggesting they are experiencing secondary resource pressure effects from the broader incident rather than being causal. No deployment or config change data is available in the provided telemetry, so a specific code change trigger cannot be confirmed; however, the memory growth pattern in adservice is consistent with a memory leak introduced by a recent deployment or a sudden traffic surge causing OOM-adjacent behavior."}

{"root cause occurrence datetime": "2024-11-18 07:25:32", "root cause component": "emailservice", "root cause reason": "The investigation reveals two distinct failure signals that together point to emailservice as the primary root cause of the checkout pipeline degradation, with paymentservice as a contributing secondary fault. The earliest anomalous signal chain begins with frontend reporting an elevated error rate (0.1) at 07:15:20, coinciding with productcatalogservice showing high workload (70.0) at the same timestamp \u2014 suggesting user-facing load was already stressing downstream services. However, the most actionable failure cluster centers on checkoutservice, which logged repeated 'rpc error: code = Unimplemented desc = Method not found!' errors when attempting to send order confirmations between 07:32:39\u201307:32:55. The gRPC 'Unimplemented' status code is definitive: it means checkoutservice is calling a method on emailservice that does not exist in the currently deployed version of emailservice. This is a classic symptom of a breaking API contract change \u2014 either emailservice was rolled back to an older version that does not implement the expected RPC method, or a new version of checkoutservice was deployed that calls a method not yet present in emailservice. The emailservice log at 07:25:32 confirms it was receiving order confirmation requests but its elevated memory (88MB at 07:30:51) and subsequent RPC failures suggest a version mismatch introduced around that window. The paymentservice error ('UnacceptedCreditCard: visa_electron not accepted') at 07:31:58 is an independent application-level bug \u2014 a missing card type in the payment processor's allowlist \u2014 and contributes to checkout failures but is a separate fault vector. The broad memory pressure across nearly all services (cartservice, currencyservice, recommendationservice, redis-cart, shippingservice, adservice) is consistent with cascading retry storms triggered by the checkout pipeline failures propagating back to frontend. Root cause is therefore a broken emailservice deployment (wrong version or misconfigured gRPC service definition) that caused the 'Method not found' contract violation, which blocked order completion and propagated errors upstream through checkoutservice to frontend."}

{"root cause occurrence datetime": "2024-11-22 14:29:39", "root cause component": "emailservice", "root cause reason": "The failure chain is anchored to the emailservice based on the following evidence: (1) The earliest meaningful anomalous event in the timeline is emailservice receiving an order confirmation request at 14:29:39, followed by checkoutservice beginning to emit a series of 'rpc error: code = Unimplemented desc = Method not found!' errors starting at 14:39:34 when attempting to send order confirmations. The 'Unimplemented' gRPC status code strongly indicates that the emailservice has a method that checkoutservice is attempting to call which is no longer registered or available \u2014 a classic symptom of a bad deployment where a gRPC service definition was changed or a method was removed/renamed without backward compatibility. (2) The checkoutservice errors are deterministic and repeating across multiple unique customers over a ~34 second window, indicating a systemic RPC contract breakage rather than transient network issues. (3) The emailservice memory reading at 14:38:28 (88326144 bytes, ~84MB) is notably elevated compared to other lightweight services, potentially consistent with a new build artifact being loaded. (4) The cascade propagation is as follows: emailservice deploys a breaking gRPC interface change \u2192 checkoutservice fails to complete order flows \u2192 checkout failures propagate upstream causing frontend elevated error rates (0.1 at 14:33:23) and memory pressure across dependent services (cartservice, recommendationservice, adservice, etc.) as requests back up or retry. (5) redis-cart shows early disk I/O anomaly at 14:25:03 and currencyservice/shippingservice show memory anomalies at 14:27-14:28, which may reflect a rolling deployment window that began slightly earlier, but the definitive failure signal is the RPC method-not-found error pattern originating from emailservice's interface.
